narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors, estimated a 65% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first attempt. This prediction stems from a careful analysis of your profile as a Black male applicant, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.30, LSAT score of 39, Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, upper-middle family income, and planned full-time enrollment. While your LSAT score stands out positively above the typical 36.70 average, and your GPA is slightly stronger than the average 3.21, other elements tipped the scales toward concern. The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure was your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, well below the average of 5.53, signaling a notable dip in performance relative to peers that raised red flags about sustained academic momentum. Next in importance, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success by demonstrating solid reasoning and analytical skills compared to most applicants. Your Year 1 decile of 3, though lower than the average 5.71, had a milder but still negative influence, contributing somewhat to the failure risk by highlighting an early academic position not as competitive as many who pass the bar. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by having strengths in test scores and background that align with or exceed averages, but your decile rankings place you below the stronger performers who generally succeed on the first try. These factors combined to create enough doubt about bar passage that the model recommended rejection to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it even higher or addressing your decile weaknesses through targeted academic improvement, perhaps via additional coursework or test prep that showcases upward trends. Reapplying after building a stronger recent record could shift future predictions in your favor, and many applicants in similar positions succeed by focusing on these areas. Your upper-middle background and full-time commitment are assets that align with successful profiles, so leveraging them strategically will help. Keep pushing—your potential is evident, and with refinements, law school doors can reopen.
